title: Arrays and Lists
summary: Store and manage collections of data using arrays

sections.title: Arrays Are Like Numbered Lockers
sections.content: An array is like a row of lockers, each with a numbered door. You store a value in locker 0, another in locker 1, and so on. In JavaScript, arrays can hold any type — strings, numbers, even other arrays. The position number is called the index, and it always starts at 0, not 1. This zero-based indexing is one of the first things every programmer must memorize, like remembering that elevator buttons for the ground floor say 0 in many countries.

sections.title: Creating and working with arrays
sections.content: Builds a shopping list array and shows common array operations

sections.title: Looping Through Arrays
sections.content: The real power of arrays comes when you combine them with loops. Instead of writing fruits[0], fruits[1], fruits[2] manually, you write a loop that goes from index 0 to fruits.length - 1. The loop variable i acts as the index, and fruits[i] gives you each element in sequence. This pattern — looping over an array — is one of the most common operations in programming, used everywhere from displaying shopping lists to processing data.

sections.title: Off-by-one errors with array indexes
sections.content: The #1 mistake beginners make is forgetting that array indexes start at 0, not 1. Wrong: fruits[1] gives 'banana' thinking it is the first element. Correct: fruits[0] gives 'apple'. Also, using <= fruits.length in a loop accesses fruits[fruits.length] which is undefined (indexes go from 0 to length-1). Correct loop: for (let i = 0; i < fruits.length; i++).

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: You are tracking daily temperatures for a week. Create an array with these values: 72, 75, 68, 70, 73, 71, 69. Write a loop that calculates and prints the average temperature for the week.
hints: How do you add each temperature to a running total inside the loop?
What is the relationship between the sum of all temperatures and the array length?
After the loop finishes, where should you calculate and print the average — inside or outside the loop?
